title: Differentiability in Quadratic Mean
informal: A parametric model $M$ is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta$ with score $\ell$ relative to the dominating measure $\mu$ if the Hellinger residual lies in $L^2(\mu)$ for $h$ near $0$ and the square-root densities admit the $L^2(\mu)$ expansion $$\int\Big(\sqrt{p_{\theta+h}}-\sqrt{p_\theta}-\tfrac12\langle h,\ell\rangle\sqrt{p_\theta}\Big)^2\,d\mu = o(\|h\|^2)$$ as $h\to0$.
summary: A parametric model is differentiable in quadratic mean at theta with score ell when the Hellinger residual of the square-root densities is o-of-norm-h-squared in L2.
leanSignature:
structure DifferentiableQuadraticMean
    (M : ParametricFamily 𝓧 Θ) (μ : Measure 𝓧) (θ : Θ)
    (ℓ : 𝓧 → Θ) : Prop where
  mem : ∀ᶠ h in 𝓝 (0 : Θ),
    MemLp (fun x => M.sqrtDensity (θ + h) x - M.sqrtDensity θ x
                    - (1/2 : ℝ) * ⟪h, ℓ x⟫ * M.sqrtDensity θ x) 2 μ
  isLittleO :
    (fun h : Θ =>
      ∫ x, (M.sqrtDensity (θ + h) x
            - M.sqrtDensity θ x
            - (1/2 : ℝ) * ⟪h, ℓ x⟫ * M.sqrtDensity θ x) ^ 2 ∂μ)
    =o[𝓝 (0 : Θ)] (fun h : Θ => ‖h‖ ^ 2)
formalizationNotes: The structure adds the field `mem` (residual eventually in L2(mu)) which is implicit in the Lebesgue-integral formulation but must be stated explicitly because Lean's Bochner integral assigns value 0 to non-integrable functions; the explicit L2-membership conjunct makes the formal statement equivalent to the Lebesgue-integral version.

hypotheses.label: L2 residual
hypotheses.note: The Hellinger residual is in L2(mu) for h in a neighbourhood of 0; recovers the finiteness implicit in vdV's Lebesgue integral.

hypotheses.label: o(‖h‖²) rate
hypotheses.note: The integrated squared Hellinger residual is little-o of ‖h‖² as h → 0, i.e. vdV Eq (7.1).

title: Quadratic-Mean-Differentiable Path
informal: A dominated quadratic-mean-differentiable path at a probability measure $P$ is a curve $t\mapsto Q_t$ of probability measures with $Q_0=P$, each dominated by a fixed $\sigma$-finite measure $\mu$, together with a score $g\in L^2_0(P)$ (mean zero, square-integrable) such that $$\big\|\sqrt{p_t}-\sqrt{p_0}-\tfrac{t}{2}\,g\,\sqrt{p_0}\big\|_{L^2(\mu)}\big/|t|\to0$$ as $t\to0$, where the densities $p_t$ are Radon–Nikodym derivatives of $Q_t$ with respect to $\mu$.
summary: A curve of probability measures through P, dominated by a fixed measure, with an L2-zero-mean score whose half is the L2 derivative of the square-root density.
leanSignature:
structure QMDPath (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P] where
  curve : ℝ → Measure Ω
  curve_at_zero : curve 0 = P
  curve_isProbability : ∀ t, IsProbabilityMeasure (curve t)
  dominating : Measure Ω
  curve_absContinuous : ∀ t, curve t ≪ dominating
  dominating_sigmaFinite : SigmaFinite dominating
  score : ↥(L2ZeroMean P)
  qmd_limit :
    Tendsto
      (fun t : ℝ =>
        eLpNorm (fun ω : Ω =>
          Real.sqrt ((curve t).rnDeriv dominating ω).toReal
            - Real.sqrt ((curve 0).rnDeriv dominating ω).toReal
            - (t / 2) * (score : Ω → ℝ) ω
                * Real.sqrt ((curve 0).rnDeriv dominating ω).toReal)
          2 dominating / ENNReal.ofReal |t|)
      (𝓝[≠] 0) (𝓝 (0 : ℝ≥0∞))
formalizationNotes: Formalizes the dominated specialisation: a fixed sigma-finite dominating measure and densities via Radon–Nikodym derivatives. The score's type ↥(L2ZeroMean P) bakes in mean-zero + square-integrability. The qmd_limit is stated in ℝ≥0∞-quotient form so it genuinely forces the residual into L²(μ) near 0, rather than being vacuously satisfied by the .toReal form.

hypotheses.label: QMD limit
hypotheses.note: The L²(μ) norm of the square-root-density residual, divided by |t|, tends to 0 along the punctured neighbourhood of 0.

hypotheses.label: passes through P
hypotheses.note: The curve of probability measures equals P at t = 0.

hypotheses.label: dominated
hypotheses.note: Every measure on the curve is absolutely continuous w.r.t. the fixed dominating measure.

hypotheses.label: L²₀ score
hypotheses.note: The score lives in L²₀(P): mean zero and square-integrable, the analytic content of vdV lem 25.14.

title: Regular Estimator Sequence
informal: An estimator sequence $T_n$ for $\psi(\theta)$ is regular at $\theta_0$ if there exists a limit probability measure $L_\theta$ on $\mathbb{R}^d$ such that for every $h\in\mathbb{R}^k$, $$\sqrt n\,\big(T_n-\psi(\theta_0+h/\sqrt n)\big)\xrightarrow{P^n_{\theta_0+h/\sqrt n}} L_\theta,$$ with the limit $L_\theta$ the same for every $h$. The independence of $L_\theta$ from $h$ is the regularity requirement.
summary: An estimator sequence whose rescaled recentred statistic converges weakly to one common h-independent limit law under each local alternative.
leanSignature:
structure RegularEstimatorSequence
    (M : ParametricFamily 𝓧 (AsymptoticRepresentation.Θ k)) (μ : Measure 𝓧)
    (θ₀ : AsymptoticRepresentation.Θ k)
    (ψ : AsymptoticRepresentation.Θ k → AsymptoticRepresentation.𝓨 d)
    (T : ∀ n, (Fin n → 𝓧) → AsymptoticRepresentation.𝓨 d) : Type where
  limitDist : Measure (AsymptoticRepresentation.𝓨 d)
  isProb : IsProbabilityMeasure limitDist
  tendsto : ∀ h : AsymptoticRepresentation.Θ k,
    WeakConverges
      (fun n : ℕ =>
        (AsymptoticRepresentation.productMeasure M μ (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h) n).map
          (fun x => (Real.sqrt n) •
            (T n x - ψ (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h))))
      limitDist
formalizationNotes: `isProb` records the explicit 'probability measure' qualifier on L_θ. Weak convergence uses the project's WeakConverges test-function form. The estimator T is a sequence of measurable maps on the n-fold product space.

hypotheses.label: regular convergence
hypotheses.note: For every h, √n(T_n − ψ(θ₀+h/√n)) converges weakly to limitDist under the local alternative P^n_{θ₀+h/√n}.

hypotheses.label: common limit law
hypotheses.note: The single h-independent limit distribution L_θ; its h-independence is exactly regularity.

title: Bowl-Shaped Loss Function
informal: A loss function $L:E\to[0,\infty]$ is bowl-shaped if it is measurable, symmetric about the origin ($L(-x)=L(x)$), and has convex sublevel sets $\{x : L(x)\le c\}$. Combined, symmetry and convex sublevel sets give the defining "convex symmetric sublevel sets".
summary: A measurable, origin-symmetric loss with convex sublevel sets; the standing loss assumption for the local asymptotic minimax theorem.
leanSignature:
structure BowlShaped [AddCommGroup E] [Module ℝ E] [MeasurableSpace E]
    (L : E → ℝ≥0∞) : Prop where
  measurable : Measurable L
  symm : ∀ x, L (-x) = L x
  convex_sublevel : ∀ c : ℝ≥0∞, Convex ℝ {x | L x ≤ c}
formalizationNotes: This is bowl-shaped only, not subconvex: the latter is reserved for bowl-shaped with closed sublevel sets (lower semicontinuous), which this structure does not require — there is no lsc/closed-sublevel field. Measurability is kept as an explicit field (implicit in the original) so the structure works for E more general than ℝ^d. Codomain ℝ≥0∞ (the original uses [0,∞)) matches Mathlib's risk framework and is a no-op for finite-valued losses.

hypotheses.label: measurable
hypotheses.note: L is measurable; implicit in vdV but stated explicitly so it holds for general E.

hypotheses.label: symmetric
hypotheses.note: L is symmetric about the origin: L(-x) = L(x).

hypotheses.label: convex sublevels
hypotheses.note: Every sublevel set {x | L x ≤ c} is convex; with symmetry this is 'convex symmetric sublevel sets'.

title: Gaussian Shift Experiment
informal: A family $h\mapsto \text{family}(h)$ of measures on $\mathbb{R}^k$ is a Gaussian shift with covariance $\Sigma$ if each $\text{family}(h)$ is a Gaussian probability measure with mean $h$ and covariance the positive-definite matrix $\Sigma$ — i.e. $\text{family}(h)=N(h,\Sigma)$, where $\Sigma$ is required positive-definite. This is the limit experiment to which the local family $P^n_{\theta_0+h/\sqrt n}$ is weakly equivalent.
summary: The family h to N(h, Sigma) of Gaussian probability measures with mean h and fixed positive-definite covariance Sigma, the limit experiment of local asymptotic normality.
leanSignature:
structure IsGaussianShift
    (family : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k) → Measure (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)))
    (cov : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ) : Prop where
  posDef : Matrix.PosDef cov
  isProb : ∀ h, IsProbabilityMeasure (family h)
  isGaussian : ∀ h, IsGaussian (family h)
  mean_eq (h u : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)) :
    (∫ x, ⟪u, x⟫ ∂(family h)) = ⟪u, h⟫
  cov_eq (h u v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)) :
    (∫ x, ⟪u, x - h⟫ * ⟪v, x - h⟫ ∂(family h)) =
      ⟪u, (WithLp.equiv 2 _).symm (cov.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 _) v))⟫
formalizationNotes: Packaged as a Prop predicate on a measure family with separate fields for Gaussianity, mean, and covariance (stated via inner-product integrals) because Mathlib lacks a direct multivariateGaussian primitive at this commit. The closed-form log-likelihood ratio is recorded separately as HasGaussianShiftLogLikelihoodRatio.

hypotheses.label: positive-definite cov
hypotheses.note: The covariance matrix Σ is positive-definite.

hypotheses.label: Gaussian
hypotheses.note: Each member of the family is a Gaussian probability measure.

hypotheses.label: mean h
hypotheses.note: family(h) has mean h, stated via ∫⟨u,x⟩ = ⟨u,h⟩ for all u.

hypotheses.label: covariance Σ
hypotheses.note: family(h) has covariance Σ, via the centred bilinear integral.

title: Equivariant-in-Law Estimator
informal: A randomized estimator $T$ of $A\cdot h$ in the Gaussian shift experiment, encoded as a Markov kernel $\mathbb{R}^k\rightsquigarrow\mathbb{R}^d$, is equivariant-in-law if for every $h$, the distribution of $T(X)-A\cdot h$ under $X\sim N(h,S)$ equals one common probability measure $L$ — i.e. the law of $T-Ah$ does not depend on $h$. That common $L$ is the null distribution of $T$.
summary: A randomized estimator (Markov kernel) whose centred law T(X) − Ah under N(h,S) is the same probability measure for every shift h.
leanSignature:
structure IsEquivariantInLaw
    (T : Kernel (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)) (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d)))
    (A : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin k) ℝ)
    (S : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ) : Prop where
  invariant_law :
    ∃ L : Measure (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d)),
      IsProbabilityMeasure L ∧
      ∀ h : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k),
        (T ∘ₘ (multivariateGaussian h S)).map
            (fun y => y - (WithLp.equiv 2 (Fin d → ℝ)).symm
              (A.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 (Fin k → ℝ)) h)))
          = L
formalizationNotes: The estimator is encoded as a Markov kernel; the law of T(X) is T ∘ₘ N(h,S), pushed forward by the translation y ↦ y − Ah. The single field invariant_law Skolemizes the common null distribution L (accessor IsEquivariantInLaw.nullDist). The covariance written Σ in the literature is named S in Lean, since Σ is reserved.

hypotheses.label: h-invariant centred law
hypotheses.note: There is one probability measure L equal to the pushforward of T∘N(h,S) by y ↦ y − Ah for every h.

title: Parametric Submodel is Differentiable in Quadratic Mean
informal: The finite-dimensional parametric submodel constructed from a bounded family of mixture scores $g_P$ that are orthonormal in $L^2(P)$ is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta=0$ with multi-dimensional score $g_{P,\text{total}}$: the integrated squared Hellinger residual $\int\big(\sqrt{p_\theta}-1-\tfrac12\langle\theta,g_{P,\text{total}}\rangle\big)^2$ is $o(\|\theta\|^2)$ as $\theta\to0$.
summary: The submodel built from bounded orthonormal mixture scores is differentiable in quadratic mean at zero with score g_P_total.
leanSignature:
theorem paramSubmodel_DQM :
    DifferentiableQuadraticMean (paramSubmodel g_P hg h_orth) P 0 (g_P_total g_P)
formalizationNotes: The score family g_P, its boundedness hg (IsBoundedMixtureScores) and L²(P)-orthonormality h_orth are section variables, not explicit binders in the signature line. DQM is proven at θ = 0 for the SubmodelFromScores construction. The rate follows from the Taylor inequality |√(1+u)−1−u/2| ≤ u²/2 for 0 ≤ 1+u together with the uniform bound on the perturbation, giving an O(‖θ‖⁴) integrated squared residual, hence o(‖θ‖²).

hypotheses.label: bounded mixture scores
hypotheses.note: The submodel is built from the score family g_P (a section variable carried into the statement).

hypotheses.label: orthonormal scores
hypotheses.note: The scores g_P are orthonormal in L²(P); this gives the Fisher information identity and underlies the DQM proof.

title: Local Asymptotic Normality Expansion
informal: Let $(P_\theta)$ be a parametric family that is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta_0$ with score $\ell$, with $p_\theta$ a genuine $\mu$-density (normalising and integrable for every $\theta$), and let $X_i$ be i.i.d. with law $P_{\theta_0}$. Then for any $h_n\to h$, the score has zero mean, the Fisher information is finite in every direction, and the log-likelihood ratio admits the LAN expansion $$\log\prod_i\frac{p_{\theta_0+h_n/\sqrt n}(X_i)}{p_{\theta_0}(X_i)}=\frac1{\sqrt n}\sum_i\langle h,\ell(X_i)\rangle-\tfrac12\langle h,I_{\theta_0}h\rangle+o_P(1).$$
summary: Under DQM at theta0 with iid data and a converging local parameter sequence, the log-likelihood ratio of local alternatives expands as a score average minus half the Fisher quadratic form plus o_P(1).
leanSignature:
theorem LAN_expansion
    {k : ℕ}
    {Ω : Type*} {mΩ : MeasurableSpace Ω} (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    (M : ParametricFamily 𝓧 (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k))) (μ : Measure 𝓧)
    [SigmaFinite μ]
    (θ₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k))
    (ℓ : 𝓧 → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)) (hℓ : Measurable ℓ)
    (hPDF : IsPDFOf M μ)
    (hDQM : DifferentiableQuadraticMean M μ θ₀ ℓ)
    (h : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)) (h_n : ℕ → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k))
    (hconv : Filter.Tendsto h_n Filter.atTop (𝓝 h))
    (X : ℕ → Ω → 𝓧) (hX_meas : ∀ i, Measurable (X i))
    (hindep : Pairwise fun i j => ProbabilityTheory.IndepFun (X i) (X j) P)
    (hident : ∀ i, ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0) P P)
    (hlaw : Measure.map (X 0) P
              = μ.withDensity fun x => ENNReal.ofReal (M.density θ₀ x)) :
    (∀ u : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k), ∫ x, ⟪u, ℓ x⟫ * M.density θ₀ x ∂μ = 0) ∧
    (∀ u : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k),
      Integrable (fun x => ⟪u, ℓ x⟫ ^ 2 * M.density θ₀ x) μ) ∧
    TendstoInMeasure P
      (fun n ω =>
        (∑ i ∈ Finset.range n,
          Real.log (M.density (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h_n n) (X i ω)
                    / M.density θ₀ (X i ω)))
        - (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ * ∑ i ∈ Finset.range n, ⟪h, ℓ (X i ω)⟫
        + (1/2 : ℝ) * fisherInformation M μ θ₀ ℓ h h)
      Filter.atTop (fun _ => (0 : ℝ))
formalizationNotes: The o_P(1) remainder is encoded as TendstoInMeasure to 0. The conclusion bundles three claims: score mean zero (i), finite Fisher information (ii), and the LAN expansion (iii). hPDF (IsPDFOf) packages that p_θ normalises and is integrable for every θ — the tacit assumption in writing p_θ. The iid sample is given by explicit pairwise-independence, identical-distribution, and law fields.

hypotheses.label: genuine density
hypotheses.note: IsPDFOf M μ: p_θ is a μ-density that normalises to 1 and is integrable for every θ; vdV's tacit assumption.

hypotheses.label: DQM at θ₀
hypotheses.note: The family is differentiable in quadratic mean at θ₀ with score ℓ — the central regularity condition.

hypotheses.label: h_n → h
hypotheses.note: The local parameter sequence h_n converges to h.

hypotheses.label: iid sample
hypotheses.note: Together with hindep and hlaw, the X_i are i.i.d. with law P_{θ₀}.

title: Contiguity of Local Alternatives
informal: Suppose the family is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta_0$ with score $\ell$ and non-singular Fisher information $J$, and that the log-likelihood ratio of the local alternative converges weakly under $P^n_{\theta_0}$ to $N(-v/2, v)$ (with $v=\langle h,Jh\rangle$), where $P^n_{\theta_0+h/\sqrt n}$ is the measure with density proportional to the likelihood ratio, i.e. $P^n_{\theta_0}$ reweighted by the exponential of the log-likelihood ratio. Then by Le Cam's first lemma the sequences $P^n_{\theta_0}$ and $P^n_{\theta_0+h/\sqrt n}$ are mutually contiguous.
summary: Under DQM and asymptotic log-normality of the local likelihood ratio, the local alternatives and the base product measures are mutually contiguous (Le Cam's first lemma).
leanSignature:
theorem contiguous_local_alternatives
    (M : ParametricFamily 𝓧 (Θ k)) (μ : Measure 𝓧) [SigmaFinite μ]
    [∀ θ : Θ k, ∀ n, IsProbabilityMeasure (productMeasure M μ θ n)]
    (θ₀ : Θ k) (ℓ : 𝓧 → Θ k) (_hℓ : Measurable ℓ)
    (_hDQM : DifferentiableQuadraticMean M μ θ₀ ℓ)
    (J : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ) (_hJ_pd : Matrix.PosDef J)
    (_hJ : ∀ u v : Θ k, fisherInformation M μ θ₀ ℓ u v =
      ⟪u, (WithLp.equiv 2 _).symm (J.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 _) v))⟫)
    (h : Θ k)
    (hL_meas : ∀ n : ℕ, Measurable (logLikelihood M θ₀ h n))
    (hL_is_log_ratio : ∀ n : ℕ,
        productMeasure M μ (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h) n =
          (productMeasure M μ θ₀ n).withDensity
            (fun ω => ENNReal.ofReal (Real.exp (logLikelihood M θ₀ h n ω))))
    (v : NNReal)
    (h_log_weak :
      WeakConverges (fun n => (productMeasure M μ θ₀ n).map (logLikelihood M θ₀ h n))
        (ProbabilityTheory.gaussianReal (-(v : ℝ) / 2) v)) :
    Contiguity.MutuallyContiguous (ι := ℕ)
      (Ω := fun n => Fin n → 𝓧) atTop
      (fun n => productMeasure M μ θ₀ n)
      (fun n => productMeasure M μ (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h) n)
formalizationNotes: The asymptotic log-normality of the likelihood ratio (the consequence of DQM) is the operative input. The DQM and Fisher hypotheses (_hDQM, _hJ_pd, _hJ) are carried but the proof reduces to the abstract Le Cam first lemma packaged as mutuallyContiguous_of_asymptotically_log_normal, fed the operative inputs hL_is_log_ratio (change of measure) and h_log_weak (weak convergence of the log-ratio to N(-v/2, v)).

hypotheses.label: DQM at θ₀
hypotheses.note: The family is differentiable in quadratic mean at θ₀ with score ℓ.

hypotheses.label: non-singular Fisher
hypotheses.note: The Fisher information matrix J is positive-definite.

hypotheses.label: log-ratio → N(-v/2, v)
hypotheses.note: The local log-likelihood ratio converges weakly under P^n_{θ₀} to the Gaussian N(-v/2, v).

hypotheses.label: change of measure
hypotheses.note: The local alternative is the base product measure reweighted by exp of the log-likelihood.

title: Regularity Supplies the (8.2) Weak-Convergence Input
informal: A regular estimator sequence $T_n$ for $\psi(\theta)$ at $\theta_0$ supplies the weak-convergence input required by the asymptotic representation / lower-bound machinery: for every $h$, $$\sqrt n\,\big(T_n-\psi(\theta_0+h/\sqrt n)\big)\xrightarrow{P^n_{\theta_0+h/\sqrt n}} L_\theta,$$ where the $h$-indexed family of limit laws is the constant family $h\mapsto L_\theta$ (regularity makes $L_\theta$ independent of $h$, so this is exactly the unfolding of the definition).
summary: A regular estimator sequence yields, for every h, weak convergence of the rescaled recentred statistic to the constant limit law required by the convolution-theorem lower bound.
leanSignature:
theorem regularity_implies_8_2_hypothesis
    (M : ParametricFamily 𝓧 (Θ k)) (μ : Measure 𝓧)
    (θ₀ : Θ k)
    (ψ : Θ k → 𝓨 d)
    (T : ∀ n, (Fin n → 𝓧) → 𝓨 d)
    (hReg : RegularEstimatorSequence M μ θ₀ ψ T) :
    ∀ h : Θ k,
      WeakConverges
        (fun n : ℕ =>
          (productMeasure M μ (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h) n).map
            (fun x => (Real.sqrt n) •
              (T n x - ψ (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h))))
        hReg.limitDist
formalizationNotes: A local-glue lemma feeding the §8.2 lower-bound input: it unfolds RegularEstimatorSequence.tendsto, choosing the h-indexed limit family as the constant family fun _ => hReg.limitDist. The proof body is exactly hReg.tendsto.

hypotheses.label: regular estimator
hypotheses.note: T is a regular estimator sequence for ψ at θ₀; its defining weak-convergence clause is the (8.2) input.

title: Asymptotic Representation Theorem (LAN)
informal: Assume the family is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta_0$ with score $\ell$, with non-singular Fisher information $J$ and $p_\theta$ a genuine $\mu$-density, and that a statistic sequence $T_n$ converges weakly to $L_h$ under each local alternative $P^n_{\theta_0+h/\sqrt n}$. Then there exists a Markov kernel $\kappa$ such that for every $h$, $$L_h = N(h, J^{-1})\,\mathbin{\gg\!=}\,\kappa,$$ i.e. every limit law is the image of the Gaussian shift through one fixed Markov kernel. This is the asymptotic-representation form: matching estimators in the LAN sequence to the Gaussian limit experiment.
summary: Under DQM with non-singular Fisher information, any weakly converging statistic sequence's limit laws factor as N(h, J-inverse) bound through a single Markov kernel.
leanSignature:
theorem LAN_representation_vdV
    (M : ParametricFamily 𝓧 (Θ k)) (μ : Measure 𝓧) [SigmaFinite μ]
    (θ₀ : Θ k) (ℓ : 𝓧 → Θ k) (hℓ : Measurable ℓ)
    (hDQM : DifferentiableQuadraticMean M μ θ₀ ℓ)
    (J : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ) (hJ_pd : Matrix.PosDef J)
    (hJ : ∀ u v : Θ k, fisherInformation M μ θ₀ ℓ u v =
      ⟪u, (WithLp.equiv 2 _).symm (J.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 _) v))⟫)
    (T : ∀ n, (Fin n → 𝓧) → 𝓨 d) (hT_meas : ∀ n, Measurable (T n))
    (L : Θ k → Measure (𝓨 d)) [∀ h, IsProbabilityMeasure (L h)]
    (hT_weak : ∀ h : Θ k,
      WeakConverges
        (fun n => (productMeasure M μ (θ₀ + (Real.sqrt n)⁻¹ • h) n).map (T n)) (L h))
    [StandardBorelSpace (𝓨 d)] [Nonempty (𝓨 d)]
    [HasOuterApproxClosed (𝓨 d)] [BorelSpace (𝓨 d)]
    (hPDF : IsPDFOf M μ) :
    ∃ κ : Kernel (Θ k) (𝓨 d), IsMarkovKernel κ ∧
      ∀ h : Θ k, L h = (ProbabilityTheory.multivariateGaussian h J⁻¹).bind κ
formalizationNotes: LAN_representation_vdV is a thin compatibility alias delegating to LAN_representation; the paper-literal name is LAN_representation. Standard-Borel / Borel / outer-approximation instances on the value space are encoding requirements for the kernel disintegration. The LAN/Le Cam transfer routes through DQM-derived singular-mass control, so no common-support hypothesis is needed.

hypotheses.label: DQM at θ₀
hypotheses.note: The family is differentiable in quadratic mean at θ₀ with score ℓ.

hypotheses.label: non-singular Fisher
hypotheses.note: The Fisher information matrix J is positive-definite.

hypotheses.label: weak convergence of T_n
hypotheses.note: T_n converges weakly to L_h under each local alternative P^n_{θ₀+h/√n}.

hypotheses.label: matching Markov kernel
hypotheses.note: The produced Markov kernel κ realises every L_h as N(h, J⁻¹) bound through κ.

hypotheses.label: genuine density
hypotheses.note: IsPDFOf M μ: the §7 density regularity bundle.

title: Anderson's Lemma (Set Form)
informal: Let $ u = N(0, S)$ be a centered multivariate Gaussian with positive-semidefinite covariance $S$, and let $C$ be a measurable, convex, symmetric set. Then for every shift $y$, $$ u(C-y)\le u(C),$$ i.e. by Anderson's lemma the centred set has the largest Gaussian measure among its translates.
summary: For a centered Gaussian with positive-semidefinite covariance, any translate of a convex symmetric measurable set has Gaussian measure at most that of the centred set.
leanSignature:
theorem anderson_lemma_set
    {S : Matrix ι ι ℝ} (hS : S.PosSemidef)
    {C : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ ι)} (hC_meas : MeasurableSet C)
    (hC_conv : Convex ℝ C) (hC_symm : ∀ x ∈ C, -x ∈ C)
    (y : EuclideanSpace ℝ ι) :

title: Hájek–Le Cam Convolution Theorem
informal: Suppose the experiment is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta_0$ with score $\ell$ and non-singular Fisher information $J$, the target functional $\psi$ is Fréchet-differentiable at $\theta_0$ with derivative $\dot\psi$, and $T_n$ is a regular estimator sequence for $\psi(\theta)$ with limit distribution $L_\theta$. Then by the Hájek–Le Cam convolution theorem there exists a probability measure $M_\theta$ with $$L_\theta = N\big(0,\ \dot\psi\, J^{-1}\,\dot\psi^{\mathsf T}\big) * M_\theta,$$ i.e. the limit law of any regular estimator is the convolution of the optimal Gaussian with a noise factor — so the Gaussian is the best attainable limit.
summary: The limit law of any regular estimator of psi factors as the convolution of the optimal Gaussian N(0, psidot J-inverse psidot-transpose) with some probability measure.
leanSignature:
theorem hajek_le_cam_convolution_theorem
    (M : ParametricFamily 𝓧 (Θ k)) (μ : Measure 𝓧) [SigmaFinite μ]
    (θ₀ : Θ k) (ℓ : 𝓧 → Θ k) (hℓ : Measurable ℓ)
    (hDQM : DifferentiableQuadraticMean M μ θ₀ ℓ)
    (J : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ) (hJ_pd : Matrix.PosDef J)
    (hJ_fisher : ∀ u v : Θ k, fisherInformation M μ θ₀ ℓ u v =
      ⟪u, (WithLp.equiv 2 _).symm (J.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 _) v))⟫)
    (ψ : Θ k → 𝓨 d) (ψDot : Θ k →L[ℝ] 𝓨 d)
    (hψ_diff : HasFDerivAt ψ ψDot θ₀)
    (ψDotMat : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin k) ℝ)
    (h_ψDot_mat : ∀ h : Θ k,
      ψDot h = (WithLp.equiv 2 _).symm (ψDotMat.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 _) h)))
    (T : ∀ n, (Fin n → 𝓧) → 𝓨 d) (hT_meas : ∀ n, Measurable (T n))
    (hReg : RegularEstimatorSequence M μ θ₀ ψ T)
    (hPDF : IsPDFOf M μ) :
    ∃ M_θ : Measure (𝓨 d),
      IsProbabilityMeasure M_θ ∧
      hReg.limitDist =
        (ProbabilityTheory.multivariateGaussian (0 : 𝓨 d)
          (ψDotMat * J⁻¹ * ψDotMat.transpose)) ∗ M_θ
formalizationNotes: ψDot is given both as a continuous linear map and a matrix ψDotMat (it plays both roles), bridged by h_ψDot_mat. The convolution * is the project's measure convolution. hPDF is the §7 density bundle; common-support is replaced by DQM-derived singular-mass control.

title: Local Asymptotic Minimax Bound
informal: Suppose the family is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta_0$ with score $\ell$ and non-singular Fisher information $J$, the functional $\psi$ is Fréchet-differentiable at $\theta_0$ with derivative $\dot\psi$, the loss $L$ is bowl-shaped and lower semicontinuous, and $\sqrt n\,(T_n-\psi(\theta_0))$ is uniformly tight under $P^n_{\theta_0}$. Then the local asymptotic minimax risk is bounded below by the Gaussian risk: $$\text{localAsymptoticRisk} \ge \int L\,dN\big(0,\ \dot\psi\,J^{-1}\,\dot\psi^{\mathsf T}\big).$$ No estimator can asymptotically beat the optimal Gaussian experiment.
summary: Under DQM, non-singular Fisher information, a differentiable functional, a bowl-shaped lsc loss, and tightness, the local asymptotic minimax risk is at least the optimal Gaussian risk.

theorem local_asymptotic_minimax_bound

    (θ₀ : Θ k) (ℓ : 𝓧 → Θ k)
    (hℓ : Measurable ℓ)
    (hDQM : DifferentiableQuadraticMean M μ θ₀ ℓ)
    (J : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ) (hJ : J.PosDef)
    (hJ_fisher : ∀ u v : Θ k, fisherInformation M μ θ₀ ℓ u v
      = ⟪u, (WithLp.equiv 2 _).symm (J.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 _) v))⟫)
    (ψ : Θ k → 𝓨 d)
    (ψDot : Θ k →L[ℝ] 𝓨 d)
    (hψ_diff : HasFDerivAt ψ ψDot θ₀)
    (ψDotMat : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin k) ℝ)
    (h_ψDot_mat : ∀ h : Θ k,
      ψDot h = (WithLp.equiv 2 _).symm (ψDotMat.mulVec ((WithLp.equiv 2 _) h)))
    (T : ∀ n, (Fin n → 𝓧) → 𝓨 d) (hT_meas : ∀ n, Measurable (T n))
    (L : 𝓨 d → ℝ≥0∞)
    (hL_bowl : BowlShaped L)
    (hL_lsc : LowerSemicontinuous L)
    (hTight : MeasureTheory.IsTightMeasureSet
        (Set.range (fun n : ℕ =>
          (AsymptoticRepresentation.productMeasure M μ θ₀ n).map
            (fun ω => (Real.sqrt n) • (T n ω - ψ θ₀)))))

title: Tangent set / tangent space
informal: At a probability measure $P$, a tangent set is a subset of the mean-zero square-integrable functions $L^2_0(P)$, each element being the score of a quadratic-mean-differentiable (QMD) submodel $t \mapsto P_t$ passing through $P$ at $t = 0$. The structure bundles a set of admissible score directions together with, for every member, a realizing QMD path whose score equals that direction. Closure under scaling or addition is a regularity property, not part of the definition (one works with one-sided tangent cones), so it is not imposed here. The associated tangent space is the derived closed linear span of the tangent set.
summary: A tangent set is a set of QMD-realizable score directions in L²₀(P); its closed linear span is the tangent space.
leanSignature:
structure TangentSpec (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P] where
  carrier : Set ↥(L2ZeroMean P)
  submodelOf : ∀ g ∈ carrier, ∃ γ : AsymptoticStatistics.Core.QMDPath.QMDPath P,
    γ.score = g
formalizationNotes: Separates the tangent set (this structure) from the tangent space (the derived `tangentSpace` closed-linear-span submodule). The realizing-path obligation `submodelOf` is carrier-scoped, matching the definition that each carrier element is a genuine QMD score; the L²-closure may contain limit points with no realizing path. Closure under scaling/addition is deliberately not imposed.

hypotheses.label: score-direction set
hypotheses.note: The tangent set proper: a subset of L²₀(P).

hypotheses.leanToken:
submodelOf : ∀ g ∈ carrier, ∃ γ : AsymptoticStatistics.Core.QMDPath.QMDPath P,
    γ.score = g
hypotheses.label: QMD realizability
hypotheses.note: Every carrier element is the score of a quadratic-mean-differentiable path through P.

title: Regular estimator (semiparametric)
informal: A sequence of scalar estimators $T_n$ is regular at $P$ relative to a tangent set with limit law $L$ if, for every score direction $g$ in the tangent set realized by a QMD curve, the rescaled estimator computed under the local perturbation $P_{1/\sqrt n,\,g}$ and recentered at the perturbed truth converges weakly to the same limit $L$: $$\sqrt n\,\bigl(T_n - \psi(P_{1/\sqrt n,\,g})\bigr) \rightsquigarrow L,$$ regardless of $g$ (shift-invariance). The predicate carries the functional $\psi$, its pathwise derivative and a designated efficient influence function $\tilde\psi_P$ so that the equivalent Hájek-shift form (recentered at $\psi(P)$ with deterministic shift $\langle \tilde\psi_P, g\rangle$) can be derived.
summary: An estimator is regular if the perturbed-truth-recentered rescaled statistic has the same weak limit L along every QMD score direction.
leanSignature:
def IsRegularEstimator
    (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    (T_set : TangentSpec P)
    (ψ : Measure Ω → ℝ)
    {IF_eff : ↥(L2ZeroMean P)}
    (hψ : PathwiseDifferentiableAt P (tangentSpace T_set) ψ)
    (_hEIF : IsEfficientInfluenceFunction P (tangentSpace T_set)
              hψ.derivative IF_eff)
    (T_n : ∀ n, (Fin n → Ω) → ℝ)
    (L : Measure ℝ) [IsProbabilityMeasure L] : Prop :=
  ∀ (g : ↥(L2ZeroMean P))
    (_hg : (g : ↥(L2ZeroMean P)) ∈ Submodule.span ℝ T_set.carrier)
    (curve : QMDPath P)
    (_hscore : curve.score = g),
    WeakConverges
      (fun n : ℕ =>
        (MeasureTheory.Measure.pi
            (fun _ : Fin n => curve.curve ((Real.sqrt n)⁻¹))).map
          (fun X : Fin n → Ω =>
            Real.sqrt n *
              (T_n n X - ψ (curve.curve ((Real.sqrt n)⁻¹)))))
      L
formalizationNotes: Stated in the canonical recenter-at-perturbed-truth convention so the limit `L` is direction-independent. `hEIF` and `hψ` carry no proof obligation in the definition itself; they are present so the derived Hájek-shift form (`hajek_shift_form`) can be expressed in the same predicate's data. Quantification is over the algebraic span `Submodule.span ℝ T_set.carrier`.

title: Coarsening at random
informal: A coarsening map $M : \Omega_{\mathrm{full}} \to \Omega_{\mathrm{obs}}$ is at random under the full-data law $P_{\mathrm{full}}$ if the full law factors as the bind of the observed law with a Markov kernel $\kappa$, i.e. $P_{\mathrm{full}} = (P_{\mathrm{full}}\circ M^{-1}) \mathbin{\gg\!=} \kappa$, where the kernel is concentrated on the $M$-fibres ($\kappa(y)$ lives on $\{x : M(x) = y\}$ for almost every $y$). Coarsening at random is a genuine restriction: outcome-dependent missingness is in general not at random, so theorems requiring it list it as an explicit hypothesis.
summary: Coarsening at random: the full law disintegrates over the observed law via a fibre-concentrated kernel.
leanSignature:
def IsCoarseningAtRandom
    (M : Ω_full → Ω_obs) (P_full : Measure Ω_full) : Prop :=
  ∃ κ : Kernel Ω_obs Ω_full,
    P_full = (P_full.map M).bind κ ∧
    ∀ᵐ y ∂(P_full.map M), ∀ᵐ x ∂(κ y), M x = y
formalizationNotes: Stated in the kernel-disintegration form: existence of a `ProbabilityTheory.Kernel` disintegrating `P_full` over `P_full.map M`, plus the regularity clause that the kernel concentrates on the M-fibre. For deterministic coarsenings on standard Borel spaces this is essentially automatic via Mathlib's `condDistrib`; the substantive content appears at the family level.

hypotheses.label: kernel disintegration
hypotheses.note: Full law equals the observed law bound with the kernel.

hypotheses.leanToken: ∀ᵐ y ∂(P_full.map M), ∀ᵐ x ∂(κ y), M x = y
hypotheses.label: fibre concentration
hypotheses.note: The kernel is supported on the M-fibre over y.

title: QMD score has mean zero and is square-integrable
informal: The score of a quadratic-mean-differentiable submodel is mean-zero and square-integrable. If a measurable function $g$ satisfies the QMD limit on the square-root density expansion $\bigl\| \sqrt{p_t} - \sqrt{p_0} - \tfrac{t}{2}\, g\,\sqrt{p_0}\bigr\|_{L^2(\mu)} = o(t)$ along a family of probability measures through $P$ and dominated by $\mu$, then $g$ agrees $P$-a.e. with an element of $L^2_0(P)$ — i.e. $\int g\,dP = 0$ and $g \in L^2(P)$.
summary: A function satisfying the QMD square-root-density limit is P-a.e. equal to a mean-zero, square-integrable function.
leanSignature:
theorem score_in_L2ZeroMean
    {P : Measure Ω} [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    {μ : Measure Ω} [SigmaFinite μ] (curve : ℝ → Measure Ω)
    (h_prob : ∀ t, IsProbabilityMeasure (curve t))
    (h_zero : curve 0 = P) (h_ac : ∀ t, curve t ≪ μ)
    (g : Ω → ℝ) (hg_meas : Measurable g)
    (h_qmd :
      Tendsto
        (fun t : ℝ =>
          eLpNorm (fun ω : Ω =>
            Real.sqrt ((curve t).rnDeriv μ ω).toReal
              - Real.sqrt ((curve 0).rnDeriv μ ω).toReal
              - (t / 2) * g ω
                  * Real.sqrt ((curve 0).rnDeriv μ ω).toReal)
            2 μ / ENNReal.ofReal |t|)
        (𝓝[≠] 0) (𝓝 (0 : ℝ≥0∞))) :
    ∃ g' : ↥(L2ZeroMean P), g =ᵐ[P] (g' : Ω → ℝ)
formalizationNotes: Dominated specialisation (fixed σ-finite μ, densities pt = dQt/dμ). The QMD condition is stated in ℝ≥0∞-quotient form `eLpNorm … / ENNReal.ofReal |t| → 0`, which genuinely forces square-integrability of the residual (a `.toReal²/t²` form would be vacuously satisfied when the residual leaves L²). The `IsProbabilityMeasure (curve t)` hypothesis is added — needed for the L²(μ)-norm bound on √(rnDeriv). This is the analytic part of Lemma 25.14.

title: EIF as orthogonal projection of an influence function
informal: For any influence function $\mathrm{IF}$ of a pathwise derivative $d\psi : T \to_L \mathbb{R}$ (not necessarily lying in the tangent space $T$), the orthogonal projection of $\mathrm{IF}$ onto $T$ is the efficient influence function for $d\psi$. Concretely, $\tilde\psi_P = \Pi_T \mathrm{IF}$ both represents $d\psi$ on $T$ and lies in $T$: the residual $\mathrm{IF} - \Pi_T \mathrm{IF} \in T^\perp$, so $\langle \Pi_T \mathrm{IF}, g\rangle = \langle \mathrm{IF}, g\rangle = d\psi(g)$ for every $g \in T$.
summary: Projecting any influence function onto the tangent space yields the efficient influence function.

    (hIF : IsInfluenceFunction P T dψ IF) :
    IsEfficientInfluenceFunction P T dψ (T.starProjection IF)
formalizationNotes: The `[T.HasOrthogonalProjection]` instance is the standard Hilbert-space side condition for orthogonal projection, automatic when T is closed in a complete inner-product space.

hypotheses.label: influence function
hypotheses.note: IF represents the pathwise derivative dψ on T (need not lie in T).

title: Operational efficiency bound (sup-ratio form)
informal: The supremum over the tangent space of the squared inner-product ratio equals the squared norm of the efficient influence function, $$\sup_{g \in T} \frac{\langle \tilde\psi_P, g\rangle^2}{\|g\|^2} = \|\tilde\psi_P\|^2.$$ The upper bound is Cauchy–Schwarz term-by-term; equality is attained at $g = \tilde\psi_P$, which lies in $T$ by the EIF membership clause. This is the dual face of norm-minimality among influence functions.
summary: The sup over the tangent space of ⟨ψ̃,g⟩²/‖g‖² equals ‖ψ̃‖².

title: Semiparametric convolution theorem (scalar)
informal: For a pathwise-differentiable functional $\psi$ with efficient influence function $\tilde\psi_P$ and a regular sequence of estimators with limit law $L$: (a) if $L$ has finite second moment, then the asymptotic variance is bounded below, $\|\tilde\psi_P\|^2 \le \operatorname{Var}(L)$; and (b) if the tangent set is additionally a convex cone, then $L$ decomposes as a convolution $L = N(0, \|\tilde\psi_P\|^2) * M$ for some probability measure $M$.      (∀ x ∈ T_set.carrier, ∀ y ∈ T_set.carrier,
        ∀ a b : ℝ, 0 ≤ a → 0 ≤ b → a + b = 1 →
        a • x + b • y ∈ T_set.carrier) →

title: Semiparametric convolution theorem (vector)
informal: The vector-valued convolution theorem for a $\mathbb{R}^k$-valued functional $\psi$ with efficient influence function tuple $(\tilde\psi_{P,1},\dots,\tilde\psi_{P,k})$ and a regular vector estimator with limit law $L$. (a) When $L$ has finite second moments, the asymptotic covariance matrix $\Sigma$ minus the Gram matrix $G = P\,\tilde\psi_P\tilde\psi_P^\top$ is positive semidefinite, $\Sigma - G \succeq 0$. (b) The limit law decomposes as $L = N(0, G) * M$ for some probability measure $M$.

      ∫ y, (y.ofLp i - ∫ z, z.ofLp i ∂L)
          * (y.ofLp j - ∫ z, z.ofLp j ∂L) ∂L

title: Local asymptotic minimax (vector)
informal: The vector-form local asymptotic minimax theorem. Under the convex-cone / convexity / negation-closure tangent hypotheses (which together make the tangent carrier a linear subspace) and a bowl-shaped subconvex loss $\ell$, the local asymptotic minimax risk is bounded below by the Gaussian integral: $$\sup_I \liminf_n \sup_{g \in I} \mathbb{E}_{P_{1/\sqrt n,g}}\, \ell\bigl(\sqrt n(T_n - \psi(P_{1/\sqrt n,g}))\bigr) \ge \int \ell \,dN(0, P\tilde\psi_P\tilde\psi_P^\top),$$ where the outer supremum is over finite subsets of the tangent set and the Gaussian covariance is the Gram matrix of the EIF tuple. No estimator beats the Gaussian shift limit.

theorem semiparametric_local_asymptotic_minimax_theorem

title: Operational semiparametric efficiency from asymptotic linearity
informal: The operational characterization of efficiency. If $\varphi$ is an efficient influence function for the pathwise derivative $d\psi$ at $P$ relative to $T$, and $T_n$ is asymptotically linear at $P$ with influence function $\varphi$ and centering $\psi(P)$ — i.e. $\sqrt n(T_n - \psi(P)) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt n}\sum_i \varphi(X_i) + o_P(1)$ — then $T_n$ is semiparametrically efficient at $P$ relative to $T$ for $\psi$.
summary: An estimator asymptotically linear with the EIF as influence function is semiparametrically efficient.
formalizationNotes: `AsymptoticallyLinearAt` (Eq 25.22) is stated as convergence in Pⁿ-probability of the linearization residual to zero, with the centering c kept abstract (consumers pass c = ψ P). The proof is definitional (And-introduction under the existential witnesses defining operational efficiency).

hypotheses.note: T_n has the asymptotic-linear expansion with influence φ and centering ψ(P).

title: EIF from the efficient score
informal: The efficient score $\tilde\ell_v$ in direction $v$ is the ordinary score $S_\theta v$ minus its orthogonal projection onto the nuisance tangent space; the efficient information is $\tilde I(v) = \|\tilde\ell_v\|^2$. Under nondegenerate information, the normalized efficient score $\tfrac{1}{\tilde I(v)}\,\tilde\ell_v$ is an efficient influence function for the linear-functional derivative $d\psi$, provided the candidate lies in the target tangent space and $d\psi$ acts on $T$ as $\tfrac{1}{\tilde I(v)}\langle \tilde\ell_v, \cdot\rangle$.

title: EIF via the information operator
informal: Information-operator form. Given a score operator $A : H \to_L L^2_0(P)$, if $\kappa \in H$ solves the information equation $\langle A\kappa, Av\rangle = \langle \chi, v\rangle$ for all $v$ (the inner-product encoding of $(A^*A)\kappa = \chi$), then $A\kappa$ is an efficient influence function for the derivative $d\psi$, provided $A\kappa$ lies in the tangent space $T$ and $d\psi$ acts on $T$ as $\langle A\kappa, \cdot\rangle$. title: EIF via the adjoint equation
informal: Adjoint-equation form, inner-product-certified. Given a score operator and a candidate $\varphi$, if $\varphi$ lies in the tangent space $T$ and the parameter derivative is certified as an inner product $d\psi(g) = \langle \varphi, g\rangle$ for every $g \in T$, then $\varphi$ is an efficient influence function for $d\psi$. Equivalently, $\varphi$ solves the adjoint equation $A^*\varphi = \chi$: for $g \in \operatorname{range} A$ the adjoint identity $\langle Av, \varphi\rangle = \langle v, A^*\varphi\rangle = \langle v, \chi\rangle = d\psi(Av)$ recovers the inner-product hypothesis.

title: Influence function by subtracting the nuisance-score projection

  ∀ {Ξ : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ξ] {μ : Measure Ξ} [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    {X : ℕ → Ξ → Ω},
    (∀ i, Measurable (X i)) →
    Pairwise (fun i j => ProbabilityTheory.IndepFun (X i) (X j) μ) →
    (∀ i, ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0) μ μ) →
    μ.map (X 0) = P →
    ∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, Tendsto (fun n : ℕ =>
      supNormOver F
        (fun f => (n : ℝ)⁻¹ * ∑ i ∈ Finset.range n, f (X i ω) - ∫ x, f x ∂P))
      atTop (𝓝 (0 : ℝ≥0∞))
formalizationNotes: The predicate is universally quantified over the sampling space and the iid sequence, adopting the intuitive statement without committing to a particular construction of the sample. Quantified over an arbitrary sampling space (Ξ, μ) with a pairwise-independent, identically distributed sequence X whose law is P; convergence is a.s. of the sup deviation taken in ℝ≥0∞ via supNormOver.

hypotheses.label: iid P-sample on any probability space
hypotheses.note: The sequence X is measurable, pairwise independent, identically distributed, with marginal law P.

title: Donsker class
informal: A class $\mathcal{F}$ is $P$-Donsker if the empirical process $\mathbb{G}_n f = \sqrt n (P_n - P) f$ converges weakly in $\ell^\infty(\mathcal{F})$ to a tight Gaussian limit. Equivalently, this is the conjunction of a finite-dimensional marginal CLT and asymptotic equicontinuity of the empirical process in the $L^2(P)$-semimetric.
summary: F is P-Donsker iff the marginal CLT and asymptotic equicontinuity of the empirical process both hold (Thm 18.14 characterization).
leanSignature:
def IsPDonsker (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (P : Measure Ω) : Prop :=
  IsMarginalCLT F P ∧ IsAsymptoticallyEquicontinuous F P
formalizationNotes: Adopts the marginal-CLT + asymptotic-equicontinuity characterization (Thm 18.14 (a)+(b)) as the working definition rather than weak convergence in ℓ^∞(F); splitting into these two Props avoids formalising ℓ^∞(F) as a topological space. IsMarginalCLT is here defined as ∀ f ∈ F, MemLp f 2 P (the L² integrability underpinning the covariance); IsAsymptoticallyEquicontinuous is stated in the standard random-pair (consumer) form of Vaart–Wellner §2.1.

hypotheses.label: finite-dimensional marginal CLT
hypotheses.note: Encodes L²(P) integrability of every f ∈ F, the precondition for the Gaussian covariance to be defined.

hypotheses.label: asymptotic equicontinuity
hypotheses.note: Random-pair consumer form: for measurable random functions in F with L²(P)-distance tending to 0, the empirical-process difference vanishes in probability.

title: Bracket
informal: A pair $(l, u)$ of functions is a bracket $[l, u]$ if $l \le u$ pointwise, i.e. $l(x) \le u(x)$ for every $x$. The bracket $[l,u]$ denotes the set of all functions $f$ with $l \le f \le u$. This is the underlying combinatorial unit of bracketing entropy.
summary: A bracket [l, u] is a pair of functions with l ≤ u pointwise.
leanSignature: def IsBracket (l u : Ω → ℝ) : Prop := ∀ x, l x ≤ u x

hypotheses.label: l ≤ u pointwise
hypotheses.note: The lower bound never exceeds the upper bound at any point.

title: ε-bracket in L_r(P)
informal: An $\varepsilon$-bracket in $L_r(P)$ is a bracket $[l,u]$ with $l \le u$, where $l$ and $u$ are measurable and both lie in $L_r(P)$, such that its $L_r(P)$-size $\|u - l\|_{P,r}$ is less than $\varepsilon$.
summary: An ε-bracket in L_r(P) is a measurable bracket [l,u] with l,u ∈ L_r(P) and ‖u−l‖_{P,r} < ε.
formalizationNotes: The integrability and measurability conditions are bundled into the definition so that the strong-law and post-composition arguments in the bracketing theorems can fire directly on the bracket endpoints. The size condition $P(u-l)^r < \varepsilon^r$ is equivalently expressed as eLpNorm (u−l) r P < ε. Measurability and MemLp of l and u support the strong-LLN / IndepFun.comp / IdentDistrib.comp steps downstream.

hypotheses.label: bracket with l ≤ u
hypotheses.note: Pointwise ordering l(x) ≤ u(x).

hypotheses.label: l, u measurable
hypotheses.note: Needed to post-compose the iid sequence by the bracket endpoints.

hypotheses.leanToken: MemLp l r P ∧ MemLp u r P
hypotheses.label: l, u ∈ L_r(P)
hypotheses.note: Finite L_r(P)-norms of the bracketing functions.

hypotheses.leanToken: eLpNorm (fun x => u x - l x) r P < ENNReal.ofReal ε
hypotheses.label: L_r(P)-size below ε

title: Finite ε-bracketing cover / bracketing number
informal: A class $\mathcal{F}$ admits a finite $\varepsilon$-bracketing cover in $L_r(P)$ if there is a finite collection of $\varepsilon$-brackets $[l_i, u_i]$ such that every $f \in \mathcal{F}$ lies pointwise inside some bracket $[l_i, u_i]$. This witnesses $N_{[\,]}(\varepsilon, \mathcal{F}, L_r(P)) < \infty$.
summary: F has a finite ε-bracketing cover in L_r(P): finitely many ε-brackets whose union pointwise contains every f ∈ F.
leanSignature:
def HasFiniteBracketingCover (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (ε : ℝ) (r : ℝ≥0∞) (P : Measure Ω) : Prop :=
  ∃ (k : ℕ) (l u : Fin k → Ω → ℝ),
    (∀ i, IsEpsBracket ε (l i) (u i) r P) ∧
    (∀ f ∈ F, ∃ i, ∀ x, l i x ≤ f x ∧ f x ≤ u i x)
formalizationNotes: The finite collection is encoded as a Fin k-indexed family of lower and upper bounds, matching the proof where the strong law is applied to each l_i and u_i. Existence predicate equivalent to bracketingNumber ε F r P < ⊤ (proved as bracketingNumber_lt_top_iff_HasFiniteBracketingCover). The associated count N_[](ε,F,L_r) is the noncomputable bracketingNumber valued in ℕ∞.

hypotheses.leanToken: ∀ i, IsEpsBracket ε (l i) (u i) r P
hypotheses.label: finitely many ε-brackets
hypotheses.note: A Fin k-indexed family of ε-brackets in L_r(P).

hypotheses.leanToken: ∀ f ∈ F, ∃ i, ∀ x, l i x ≤ f x ∧ f x ≤ u i x
hypotheses.label: cover F pointwise
hypotheses.note: Every f ∈ F sits inside some bracket [l_i, u_i].

title: Envelope function
informal: A function $G : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ is an envelope for a class $\mathcal{F}$ if $|f(x)| \le G(x)$ for every $f \in \mathcal{F}$ and every $x$. The envelope dominates the whole class pointwise.
summary: G is an envelope for F if |f(x)| ≤ G(x) for all f ∈ F and all x.
leanSignature:
def IsEnvelope (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (G : Ω → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∀ f ∈ F, ∀ x, |f x| ≤ G x

hypotheses.leanToken: ∀ f ∈ F, ∀ x, |f x| ≤ G x
hypotheses.label: G dominates F pointwise
hypotheses.note: Pointwise bound |f(x)| ≤ G(x) over all f ∈ F.

formalizationNotes: Its role is to control the tail behaviour of the empirical process: square-integrability of G serves as a hypothesis for Donsker-class results such as the bracketing maximal inequality (Lem 19.34).

title: Glivenko–Cantelli via finite L¹-bracketing
informal: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a class of functions that are integrable under $P$ and that admit a finite $\varepsilon$-bracketing cover in $L_1(P)$ for every $\varepsilon > 0$ (i.e. $N_{[\,]}(\varepsilon, \mathcal{F}, L_1(P)) < \infty$). Then $\mathcal{F}$ is $P$-Glivenko–Cantelli.
summary: If every f ∈ F is P-integrable and F has a finite L¹(P) ε-bracketing cover for all ε > 0, then F is P-Glivenko–Cantelli.
leanSignature:
theorem isPGlivenkoCantelli_of_finite_bracketing_L1
    (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    (h_F_int : ∀ f ∈ F, Integrable f P)
    (h_bracket : ∀ ε > 0, HasFiniteBracketingCover F ε 1 P) :
    IsPGlivenkoCantelli F P
formalizationNotes: Proof idea: for each ε the strong law is applied to every bracket endpoint, the bracket sandwich bounds the sup deviation by 2ε, and a double-limit over ε = 1/(k+1) closes the argument; this generalises the classical case. The integrability hypothesis h_F_int is the only F-side condition added: measurability is recovered via Integrable.aestronglyMeasurable.

hypotheses.leanToken: h_F_int : ∀ f ∈ F, Integrable f P
hypotheses.label: every f ∈ F is P-integrable
hypotheses.note: Supplies the integrability used in the integral comparison; measurability follows from integrability.

hypotheses.leanToken: h_bracket : ∀ ε > 0, HasFiniteBracketingCover F ε 1 P
hypotheses.label: finite L¹(P) ε-bracketing cover for every ε
hypotheses.note: Equivalent to N_[](ε, F, L₁(P)) < ∞ for all ε > 0.

title: Donsker via bracketing entropy integral
informal: Every class $\mathcal{F}$ of $P$-measurable functions whose bracketing entropy integral is finite, $J_{[\,]}(1, \mathcal{F}, L_2(P)) < \infty$, is $P$-Donsker.
summary: If F is P-measurable and J_[](1,F,L₂(P)) < ∞, then F is P-Donsker (with positivity of J at every scale and the chaining bound supplied).
leanSignature:
theorem isPDonsker_of_finite_bracketing_entropy_integral
    (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    (h_meas : ∀ f ∈ F, AEMeasurable f P)
    (h_int : bracketingEntropyIntegral 1 F P < ⊤)
    (hJ_pos : ∀ δ' : ℝ, 0 < δ' → 0 < bracketingEntropyIntegral δ' F P)
    (hChainBound_outer :
      ∀ {Ξ : Type} [MeasurableSpace Ξ] (μ : Measure Ξ) [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
        (X : ℕ → Ξ → Ω),
        ∀ (Φ : Ω → ℝ), Measurable Φ → IsEnvelope F Φ → MemLp Φ 2 P →
          ∀ {δq : ℝ}, 0 < δq → ∀ (n : ℕ),
            ∫⁻ ξ, supNormOver F
                  (fun f => empiricalProcess P n (fun i : Fin n => X i.val ξ) f) ∂μ
              ≤ ENNReal.ofReal 2 * bracketingEntropyIntegral δq F P
                + ENNReal.ofReal 2 *
                  (ENNReal.ofReal (Real.sqrt n)
                    * ∫⁻ ω, ENNReal.ofReal (|Φ ω|)
                        * Set.indicator {x | δq * Real.sqrt n < |Φ x|} 1 ω ∂P)) :
    IsPDonsker F P
formalizationNotes: Proof idea: split Donsker into the marginal CLT (obtained by bracket extraction at a finite scale plus L²-membership) and asymptotic equicontinuity (driven by the bracketing maximal inequality, Lem 19.34, with the envelope tail vanishing under truncation at δ√n). Beyond the bare statement, the Lean theorem takes two extra inputs: hJ_pos (positivity of the bracketing-entropy integral at every scale, ruling out the L²-trivial degenerate case) and hChainBound_outer (the per-scale chaining sup-norm integral bound from Maximal.lean / Lem 19.34, supplied as a hypothesis rather than rederived here).

hypotheses.leanToken: h_meas : ∀ f ∈ F, AEMeasurable f P
hypotheses.label: every f ∈ F is P-measurable
hypotheses.note: AE-measurability of each class member under P.

title: Random functions in a Donsker class
informal: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $P$-Donsker class, let $f_0 \in \mathcal{F}$ lie in $L^2(P)$, and let $\hat f_n$ be jointly measurable random functions valued in $\mathcal{F}$ whose squared $L^2(P)$-distance to $f_0$ has expectation tending to $0$. Then $\mathbb{G}_n(\hat f_n) - \mathbb{G}_n(f_0) \to_P 0$; that is, for every $\eta > 0$, $\mu\{\xi : \eta < |\mathbb{G}_n(\hat f_n \xi) - \mathbb{G}_n(f_0)|\} \to 0$.
summary: For a P-Donsker class, random functions f̂_n ∈ F that are L²(P)-consistent for f₀ give G_n(f̂_n) − G_n(f₀) →_P 0.
leanSignature:
theorem donsker_random_function_consistency
    (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    (h_donsker : IsPDonsker F P)
    (f₀ : Ω → ℝ) (_hf₀ : MemLp f₀ 2 P)
    (_hf₀_in_F : f₀ ∈ F)
    (hf₀_meas : Measurable f₀)
    {Ξ : Type} [MeasurableSpace Ξ] (μ : Measure Ξ) [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    (X : ℕ → Ξ → Ω) (hX_meas : ∀ i, Measurable (X i))
    (hX_iindep : ProbabilityTheory.iIndepFun X μ)
    (hX_idem : ∀ i, ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0) μ μ)
    (hX_law : μ.map (X 0) = P)
    (f_hat : ℕ → Ξ → (Ω → ℝ))
    (h_fhat_meas : ∀ n, Measurable (Function.uncurry (f_hat n)))
    (h_range : ∀ n ω, f_hat n ω ∈ F)
    (h_l2_int : ∀ n, MeasureTheory.Integrable
      (fun ξ => ∫ x, (f_hat n ξ x - f₀ x) ^ 2 ∂P) μ)
    (h_l2_consistent :
      Tendsto (fun n =>
        ∫ ω, (∫ x, (f_hat n ω x - f₀ x) ^ 2 ∂P) ∂μ) atTop (𝓝 0)) :
    ∀ η : ℝ, 0 < η → Tendsto (fun n =>
      μ {ξ | η < |empiricalProcess P n (fun i : Fin n => X i.val ξ) (f_hat n ξ) -
                  empiricalProcess P n (fun i : Fin n => X i.val ξ) f₀|}) atTop (𝓝 0)
formalizationNotes: Proof idea: apply the asymptotic-equicontinuity predicate directly to the random pair (f̂_n, const f₀). The L²-consistency hypothesis is stated in expectation form (E∫(f̂_n − f₀)² → 0), equivalent to the textbook →_P 0 given the L² envelope and more compact in Lean. Joint-measurability adapters (hf₀_meas, h_fhat_meas) and standard iid hypotheses on X are added to invoke the equicontinuity predicate at the random pair.

hypotheses.label: F is P-Donsker
hypotheses.note: Supplies asymptotic equicontinuity of the empirical process.

hypotheses.label: f₀ ∈ F (L²(P))
hypotheses.note: Center function lies in the class and in L²(P) (via _hf₀).

hypotheses.leanToken: h_range : ∀ n ω, f_hat n ω ∈ F
hypotheses.label: random functions valued in F
hypotheses.note: Jointly measurable f̂_n with values in F.

hypotheses.leanToken:
h_l2_consistent :
      Tendsto (fun n =>
        ∫ ω, (∫ x, (f_hat n ω x - f₀ x) ^ 2 ∂P) ∂μ) atTop (𝓝 0)
hypotheses.label: L²(P)-distance consistency
hypotheses.note: Expectation of the squared L²(P)-distance from f̂_n to f₀ tends to 0.

title: Empirical process under parameter estimation
informal: Under the structural hypotheses a Fréchet expansion, a Donsker class, and asymptotic linearity of $\hat\theta_n$ with influence function $\psi_{\theta_0}$ and an iid sample with law $P_{\theta_0}$, the rescaled “empirical minus estimated” functional $\sqrt n\,(P_n f - P_{\hat\theta_n} f)$ converges in distribution to $N(0, \sigma_f^2)$ for every $f \in \mathcal{F}$, where $\sigma_f^2 = \int (f - Pf - \dot P_\theta f \cdot \psi_{\theta_0})^2 \, dP_{\theta_0}$.
summary: Under parameter estimation √n(P_n f − P_{θ̂_n} f) →_d N(0, σ_f²) for each f ∈ F, with σ_f² the influence-adjusted variance.
leanSignature:
theorem empiricalProcess_param_estimation
    (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (P_θ : ℝ → Measure Ω) (θ₀ : ℝ)
    [IsProbabilityMeasure (P_θ θ₀)]
    (ψ_θ₀ : ↑(L2ZeroMean (P_θ θ₀)))
    {Ξ : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ξ] (μ : Measure Ξ) [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    (X : ℕ → Ξ → Ω)
    (hX_meas : ∀ i, Measurable (X i))
    (hX_iindep : ProbabilityTheory.iIndepFun X μ)
    (hX_idem : ∀ i, ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0) μ μ)
    (hX_law : μ.map (X 0) = P_θ θ₀)
    (θ_hat : ∀ n, (Fin n → Ω) → ℝ)
    (dPθ : (Ω → ℝ) → ℝ)
    (h : Theorem19_23Hyp F P_θ θ₀ ψ_θ₀ θ_hat dPθ)
    (target_aem : ∀ f ∈ F, ∀ (n : ℕ),
      AEMeasurable
        (fun ξ : Ξ => Real.sqrt n *
          ((1 / (n : ℝ)) * ∑ i : Fin n, f (X i.val ξ)
            - ∫ y, f y ∂(P_θ (θ_hat n (fun i : Fin n => X i.val ξ))))) μ) :
    ∀ f ∈ F,
      let σ_sq : ℝ :=
        ∫ x, (f x - ∫ y, f y ∂(P_θ θ₀)
                - dPθ f
                  * (((ψ_θ₀ : ↑(L2ZeroMean (P_θ θ₀))) : Lp ℝ 2 (P_θ θ₀)) : Ω → ℝ) x) ^ 2
          ∂(P_θ θ₀)
      MeasureTheory.TendstoInDistribution
        (fun (n : ℕ) (ξ : Ξ) => Real.sqrt n *
          ((1 / (n : ℝ)) * ∑ i : Fin n, f (X i.val ξ)
            - ∫ y, f y ∂(P_θ (θ_hat n (fun i : Fin n => X i.val ξ)))))
        atTop
        (id : ℝ → ℝ)
        (fun _ => μ)
        (ProbabilityTheory.gaussianReal 0 σ_sq.toNNReal)
formalizationNotes: Proof idea: combine the master decomposition (Lemma 2.12 + Fréchet expansion), the one-dimensional iid CLT, Slutsky absorption of the o_P remainder, and finiteness of the limiting variance. The structural assumptions (Fréchet differentiability, F Donsker, asymptotic linearity of θ̂_n with influence function ψ_θ₀) are bundled in the predicate Theorem19_23Hyp (the Lean docstring labels this the k=1 specialisation of Thm 19.23, cited as Thm 19.26). target_aem supplies AE-measurability of the F-indexed target sequence.

hypotheses.leanToken: h : Theorem19_23Hyp F P_θ θ₀ ψ_θ₀ θ_hat dPθ
hypotheses.label: structural parameter-estimation hypotheses
hypotheses.note: Bundles Fréchet expansion, F Donsker, and asymptotic linearity of θ̂_n with influence function ψ_θ₀.

hypotheses.label: iid sample with law P_{θ₀}
hypotheses.note: X is measurable, independent, identically distributed with marginal P_{θ₀}.

title: Bernstein's inequality for empirical processes
informal: Bernstein's inequality. For an iid sample $X_1,\dots,X_n$ from $P$ and a measurable function $f$ with $|f| \le M$ pointwise and $Pf^2 \le \sigma^2$, the empirical-process tail satisfies, for every $x > 0$, $$\mu\{|\mathbb{G}_n f| > x\} \le 2\exp\!\left(\frac{-x^2}{4(\sigma^2 + xM/\sqrt n)}\right).$$
summary: Bernstein tail bound for the empirical process: μ{|G_n f| > x} ≤ 2 exp(−x² / (4(σ² + xM/√n))) for bounded f with Pf² ≤ σ².
leanSignature:
theorem bernstein_inequality
    (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    (f : Ω → ℝ) (_hf_meas : Measurable f)
    {M σ : ℝ} (_hM : 0 ≤ M) (_hσ : 0 ≤ σ)
    (_hf_bdd : ∀ ω, |f ω| ≤ M)
    (_hf_var : ∫ ω, (f ω) ^ 2 ∂P ≤ σ ^ 2)
    {Ξ : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ξ] {μ : Measure Ξ} [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    {X : ℕ → Ξ → Ω}
    (hX_meas : ∀ i, Measurable (X i))
    (hX_iindep : ProbabilityTheory.iIndepFun X μ)
    (_hX_idem : ∀ i, ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0) μ μ)
    (hX_law : μ.map (X 0) = P)
    (n : ℕ) (_hn : 1 ≤ n) {x : ℝ} (_hx : 0 < x) :
    μ {ω : Ξ | x < |empiricalProcess P n (fun j : Fin n => X j.val ω) f|}
      ≤ ENNReal.ofReal (2 * Real.exp (-(x ^ 2) / (4 * (σ ^ 2 + x * M / Real.sqrt n))))
formalizationNotes: Proof idea: route through the standard Bernstein bound for sums of bounded iid variables plus an iid-to-P_n translation; structural assembly is delegated to MaximalBernstein.bernstein_inequality_aux. Sample is iIndepFun, identically distributed, with law P.

hypotheses.label: f bounded by M
hypotheses.note: Pointwise bound |f| ≤ M.

hypotheses.label: variance bound Pf² ≤ σ²
hypotheses.note: Second-moment control of f under P.

hypotheses.label: iid sample from P
hypotheses.note: X is measurable, iIndepFun, identically distributed with marginal P.

title: Finite-class supremum bound
informal: Finite-class supremum bound. For a finite family $g_i$ of measurable functions, each bounded by $M$ and with $Pg_i^2 \le \sigma^2$, drawn against an iid $P$-sample, there is a universal constant $K$ with $$\mathbb{E}\,\sup_i |\mathbb{G}_n g_i| \le K\left(\frac{M \log(1+|\mathcal{F}|)}{\sqrt n} + \sigma\sqrt{\log(1+|\mathcal{F}|)}\right).$$
summary: Expected sup deviation over a finite class is ≤ K(M·log(1+|F|)/√n + σ·√log(1+|F|)) for some universal K.
leanSignature:
theorem finite_sup_bound
    (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    {Ξ : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ξ] {μ : Measure Ξ} [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    {X : ℕ → Ξ → Ω}
    (hX_meas : ∀ i, Measurable (X i))
    (hX_iindep : ProbabilityTheory.iIndepFun X μ)
    (_hX_idem : ∀ i, ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0) μ μ)
    (hX_law : μ.map (X 0) = P)
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (g : ι → Ω → ℝ) (_hg_meas : ∀ i, Measurable (g i))
    {M σ : ℝ} (_hM : 0 ≤ M) (_hσ : 0 ≤ σ)
    (_hg_bdd : ∀ i ω, |g i ω| ≤ M)
    (_hg_var : ∀ i, ∫ ω, (g i ω) ^ 2 ∂P ≤ σ ^ 2)
    (n : ℕ) (_hn : 1 ≤ n) :
    ∃ K : ℝ, 0 < K ∧
      ∫⁻ ω, ENNReal.ofReal
          (⨆ i : ι, |empiricalProcess P n (fun j : Fin n => X j.val ω) (g i)|) ∂μ
        ≤ ENNReal.ofReal
            (K * (M * Real.log (1 + Fintype.card ι) / Real.sqrt n
              + σ * Real.sqrt (Real.log (1 + Fintype.card ι))))
formalizationNotes: Proof idea: build on Bernstein's inequality (Lem 19.32) via a union bound and Jensen's inequality. The constant K is existential and positive; |F| is Fintype.card ι. Structural assembly delegated to MaximalOrlicz.finite_sup_bound_aux (truncation + Jensen).

hypotheses.label: each g_i bounded by M
hypotheses.note: Uniform pointwise bound across the finite family.

hypotheses.label: variance bound Pg_i² ≤ σ²
hypotheses.note: Uniform second-moment control.

hypotheses.label: finite family of functions
hypotheses.note: g indexed by a finite type ι; |F| = Fintype.card ι.

title: Bracketing maximal inequality
informal: Bracketing maximal inequality. For a class $\mathcal{F}$ with $Pf^2 \le \delta^2$ for every $f \in \mathcal{F}$, a measurable envelope $\Phi$, and a finite bracketing entropy bound $J < \infty$, there is a constant $K$ with $$\mathbb{E}^*\|\mathbb{G}_n\|_{\mathcal{F}} \le K\left(1 + J_{[\,]}(\delta,\mathcal{F},L_2(P)) + \sqrt n\, P^*\Phi\,\mathbf{1}\{\Phi > \delta\sqrt n\}\right).$$
summary: E*‖G_n‖_F ≤ K(1 + J_[](δ,F,L₂(P)) + √n·P*Φ·1{Φ>δ√n}) for some (non-universal) K, given an envelope Φ and Pf² ≤ δ² on F.
leanSignature:
theorem maximal_inequality_bracketing
    (P : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure P]
    {Ξ : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ξ] {μ : Measure Ξ} [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    {X : ℕ → Ξ → Ω}
    (hX_meas : ∀ i, Measurable (X i))
    (_hX_iindep : ProbabilityTheory.iIndepFun X μ)
    (hX_idem : ∀ i, ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0) μ μ)
    (hX_law : μ.map (X 0) = P)
    (F : Set (Ω → ℝ)) (Φ : Ω → ℝ) (hΦ_env : IsEnvelope F Φ)
    (hΦ_meas : Measurable Φ)
    {δ : ℝ} (hδ : 0 < δ)
    (_h_var : ∀ f ∈ F, ∫ ω, (f ω) ^ 2 ∂P ≤ δ ^ 2)
    (J : ℝ≥0∞) (_hJ : J < ⊤)
    (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
    ∃ K : ℝ, 0 < K ∧
      ∫⁻ ω, supNormOver F
          (fun f => empiricalProcess P n (fun j : Fin n => X j.val ω) f) ∂μ
        ≤ ENNReal.ofReal K *
            (1 + J + ENNReal.ofReal (Real.sqrt n)
              * ∫⁻ ω, ENNReal.ofReal (|Φ ω|)

J is an arbitrary user-supplied finite upper bound on J_[](δ,F,L₂(P)). A tight universal-K variant maximal_inequality_bracketing_tight is also in the file.

title: Prékopa–Leindler inequality on ℝⁿ
informal:
The Prékopa–Leindler inequality is the functional form of Brunn–Minkowski. Let $f,g,h:\mathbb{R}^\iota\to[0,\infty]$ be measurable and let $t\in(0,1)$. If for all $x,y$ the pointwise inequality $f(x)^t\,g(y)^{1-t}\le h\big(tx+(1-t)y\big)$ holds, then the integrals against Lebesgue measure satisfy

$$\Big(\int f\Big)^{t}\Big(\int g\Big)^{1-t}\;\le\;\int h.$$

Equivalently, the marginal of a log-concave density along an affine average is itself log-concave. This is the route by which Anderson's lemma is obtained for multivariate Gaussian shifts.
summary: If f(x)^t g(y)^(1-t) ≤ h(t·x+(1-t)·y) pointwise, then (∫f)^t (∫g)^(1-t) ≤ ∫h.
leanSignature:
theorem prekopaLeindler.{u}
    {ι : Type u} [Fintype ι]
    {f g h : (ι → ℝ) → ℝ≥0∞}
    (hf_meas : Measurable f) (hg_meas : Measurable g) (hh_meas : Measurable h)
    {t : ℝ} (ht_pos : 0 < t) (ht_lt : t < 1)
    (h_le : ∀ x y : ι → ℝ,
      f x ^ t * g y ^ (1 - t) ≤ h (t • x + (1 - t) • y)) :
    (∫⁻ x, f x) ^ t * (∫⁻ y, g y) ^ (1 - t) ≤ ∫⁻ z, h z
formalizationNotes: Stated for `ι → ℝ` (the type carrying the default product `MeasureSpace`) rather than `EuclideanSpace ℝ ι`; integrals are `ℝ≥0∞`-valued `lintegral`s against `volume`. Proved by induction on `Fintype.card ι` over the 1D base case.

hypotheses.note: The exponent t lies in (0,1) (`ht_pos`, `ht_lt`).

title: Anderson's lemma, independent coordinates
informal:
Anderson's lemma (independent-shift form). Let $G\sim N(0,S)$ be a centered multivariate Gaussian on $\mathbb{R}^\iota$ with positive-semidefinite covariance $S$, and let $L$ be any bowl-shaped loss (its sublevel sets are convex and symmetric). For an independent random variable $Y$ given by an arbitrary probability measure on the same space,

$$\mathbb{E}\,L(G)\;\le\;\mathbb{E}\,L(G+Y).$$

Adding independent noise can only increase the expected bowl-shaped loss; equivalently the centered Gaussian concentrates the loss most.
summary: For centered Gaussian G~N(0,S), bowl-shaped L, and independent Y: E L(G) ≤ E L(G+Y).

    (Y : Measure (EuclideanSpace ℝ ι)) [IsProbabilityMeasure Y] :
    ∫⁻ x, L x ∂(multivariateGaussian 0 S) ≤
      ∫⁻ z, L z ∂((multivariateGaussian 0 S) ∗ Y)
formalizationNotes: Loss is `ℝ≥0∞`-valued (lintegral); `∗` is measure convolution. The proof writes the convolution as a Fubini integral and applies the loss form of Anderson's lemma fibrewise. Derived from `anderson_lemma_loss` via `Measure.lintegral_conv` and `lintegral_lintegral_swap`.

title: Le Cam's first lemma
citation: Le Cam's first lemma (van der Vaart 1998, §6.4)
informal:
Le Cam's first lemma. Let $P_n,Q_n$ be sequences of probability measures on $(\Omega_n,\mathcal{A}_n)$, and suppose the log-likelihood ratio $L_n=\log\frac{dQ_n}{dP_n}$ is measurable, with $Q_n$ obtained from $P_n$ by the change of measure $\frac{dQ_n}{dP_n}=e^{L_n}$. If under $P_n$ the ratio is asymptotically Gaussian, $L_n\overset{P_n}{\rightsquigarrow}N(-\tfrac{v}{2},v)$, then $P_n$ and $Q_n$ are mutually contiguous, $P_n\triangleleft\!\triangleright Q_n$.

The mean–variance pairing $-v/2$ and $v$ is exactly the condition $\mathbb{E}\,e^{V}=1$ that keeps the limiting likelihood ratio a probability density. The reverse direction follows by swapping $P\leftrightarrow Q$ and negating $L_n$.
summary: If log dQ_n/dP_n is asymptotically N(-v/2, v) under P_n, then P_n and Q_n are mutually contiguous.

    (L : ∀ n, Ω n → ℝ) (hL_meas : ∀ n, Measurable (L n))
    (hL_is_log_ratio : ∀ n,
        Q n = (P n).withDensity (fun ω => ENNReal.ofReal (Real.exp (L n ω))))
    (v : NNReal)
    (h_weak :
      WeakConverges (fun n => (P n).map (L n))
        (ProbabilityTheory.gaussianReal (-(v : ℝ) / 2) v)) :
    MutuallyContiguous (ι := ℕ) (Ω := Ω) atTop P Q
formalizationNotes: `MutuallyContiguous` unpacks to contiguity in both directions; `WeakConverges` is the test-function characterization of convergence in law. The change of measure is encoded as `Q n = (P n).withDensity (exp ∘ L n)`. Variance `v : NNReal`.

hypotheses.note: Each P n and Q n is a probability measure.

title: Le Cam's third lemma
citation: Le Cam's third lemma (van der Vaart 1998, §6.7)
informal:
Le Cam's third lemma. Let $(P_n)$ and $(Q_n)$ be sequences of probability measures on $(\Omega_n,\mathcal F_n)$ with $Q_n\ll P_n$, and let $L_n=\log\frac{dQ_n}{dP_n}$ be the log-likelihood ratio. Let $X_n$ be an $E$-valued random element ($E$ Polish). Suppose that under $P_n$ the pair $(X_n,L_n)$ converges in law to a limit $\pi$ on $E\times\mathbb{R}$, that $(e^{L_n})$ is uniformly integrable under $(P_n)$, and that $\int_{E\times\mathbb{R}} e^{\ell}\,\pi(dx,d\ell)=1$ with $e^{\ell}$ integrable. Then the laws of $X_n$ under $Q_n$ converge weakly to the measure $\nu$ on $E$ with $\nu(A)=\int_{E\times\mathbb{R}}\mathbf 1_A(x)\,e^{\ell}\,\pi(dx,d\ell)$; equivalently, for every bounded continuous $f:E\to\mathbb{R}$,

$$\mathbb{E}_{Q_n}[f(X_n)]=\mathbb{E}_{P_n}\big[f(X_n)e^{L_n}\big]\longrightarrow\int f(x)\,e^{\ell}\,\pi(dx,d\ell).$$

Thus $\mathcal L_{Q_n}(X_n)\rightsquigarrow \mathrm{pr}_{E*}\big(e^{\ell}\,\pi\big)$, the pushforward under the projection $\mathrm{pr}_E:E\times\mathbb{R}\to E$ of the exponentially tilted limit. Changing the base measure from $P_n$ to $Q_n$ reweights the limit law of $X_n$ by the exponential of the limiting log-likelihood.
summary: Joint weak convergence of (X_n, L_n) under P_n yields weak convergence of X_n under Q_n to the exp-tilted marginal of the limit.

    (hL_is_log_ratio : ∀ n,
        Q n = (P n).withDensity (fun ω => ENNReal.ofReal (Real.exp (L n ω))))
    (π : Measure (E × ℝ)) [IsProbabilityMeasure π]

title: Lévy continuity theorem (vector-valued)
informal:
Vector Lévy continuity step. On $\mathbb{R}^k$, suppose a probability law $L$ admits for each $m$ a Gaussian-convolution decomposition $L = N(0,\Sigma_m)\ast M_m$ with each $M_m$ a probability measure and each $\Sigma_m$ positive-semidefinite. If the covariances converge to a positive-semidefinite limit $G$, then there exists a probability measure $M$ with $L = N(0,G)\ast M$.

The Gaussian characteristic function never vanishes, so the residual characteristic functions $\widehat{L}/\widehat{N(0,\Sigma_m)}$ form a tight family; Prokhorov extracts a weak limit $M$, and the characteristic-function identity passes to the limit. This is the vector analogue of the scalar Lévy continuity argument.
summary: If L = N(0,Σ_m)∗M_m with Σ_m → G (PSD), then L = N(0,G)∗M for some probability measure M.
leanSignature:

    {k : ℕ}
    (L : Measure (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k))) [IsProbabilityMeasure L]
    (M_m : ℕ → Measure (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)))
    [∀ m, IsProbabilityMeasure (M_m m)]
    (Sigma_m : ℕ → Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ)
    (hSigma_psd : ∀ m, (Sigma_m m).PosSemidef)
    (G : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin k) ℝ) (hG_psd : G.PosSemidef)
    (hL_decomp : ∀ m,
      L = (multivariateGaussian 0 (Sigma_m m)) ∗ (M_m m))
    (hSigma_tendsto : Filter.Tendsto Sigma_m Filter.atTop (𝓝 G)) :
    ∃ M : Measure (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)),
      IsProbabilityMeasure M ∧
      L = (multivariateGaussian 0 G) ∗ M
formalizationNotes: Proof uses non-vanishing of the Gaussian characteristic function, tightness plus Prokhorov, and the characteristic-function quotient identity. Vector analogue of the scalar `ParametricBridge.levyMpass`. `∗` is measure convolution.

title: Cramér–Wold device
informal:
Cramér–Wold device. A sequence of probability measures $\mu_n$ on $\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}^m$ converges weakly to $\mu$ if every one-dimensional linear projection converges: if for each direction $(t,h)$ the pushforwards of $\mu_n$ under $p\mapsto t\,p_1+\langle h,p_2\rangle$ converge weakly to the corresponding pushforward of $\mu$, then $\mu_n\rightsquigarrow\mu$ jointly.

Joint convergence in law is determined by the convergence of all one-dimensional marginals. The proof applies the Lévy continuity theorem direction-by-direction.

hypotheses.note: Each μ_n and the limit μ are probability measures.

title: Slutsky's theorem
informal:
Slutsky's theorem (close-in-probability form). Let $X_n,Y_n:\Omega_n\to E$ be measurable random elements on varying base probability spaces $P_n$. If the laws of $X_n$ converge weakly to $\nu$, and $X_n$ and $Y_n$ are close in probability, $\mathrm{dist}(X_n,Y_n)\xrightarrow{P_n}0$, then the laws of $Y_n$ also converge weakly to $\nu$.

Replacing a sequence by one asymptotically indistinguishable from it does not change the limit law. The argument reduces to Lipschitz test functions and splits the error on $\{\mathrm{dist}<\varepsilon/2\}$ versus its complement.

title: Doob L² isometry
informal:
Doob's $L^2$ isomorphism. For a measurable map $f:\alpha\to\beta$, the subspace of $L^2(\mu)$ of functions measurable with respect to the pulled-back $\sigma$-algebra $f^{-1}(\sigma_\beta)$ is isometrically isomorphic to $L^2$ of the pushforward measure $f_*\mu$:

$$L^2_{f^{-1}(\sigma_\beta)}(\mu)\;\cong\;L^2(f_*\mu).$$

This is the abstract Doob–Dynkin identification: any square-integrable $f$-measurable random variable equals $g\circ f$ for a unique $g\in L^2(f_*\mu)$, with norm preserved. It underlies the representation of conditional expectations onto a generated $\sigma$-algebra.
summary: Isometric isomorphism between the f-measurable part of L²(μ) and L²(μ.map f).

      ≃ₗᵢ[ℝ] Lp ℝ 2 (μ.map f)
formalizationNotes: A `noncomputable def` returning a `≃ₗᵢ[ℝ]` (linear isometric equivalence). Built by chaining `lpMeasToLpTrimLie` with `lpTrimComapToLpMap`; the section variable `hf : Measurable f` carries the measurability. The pushforward $f_*\mu$ is `μ.map f` and the pulled-back $\sigma$-algebra is `MeasurableSpace.comap f`.

title: Doob-style Lᵖ identification
informal:
Doob bridge $L^2(\mathrm{trim})\cong L^2(\mathrm{map})$. For a measurable map $f:\alpha\to\beta$, the space $L^2$ of the trimmed measure $\mu$ restricted to the pulled-back $\sigma$-algebra $f^{-1}(\sigma_\beta)$ is isometrically isomorphic to $L^2$ of the pushforward measure $f_*\mu$:

$$L^2\big(\mu|_{f^{-1}(\sigma_\beta)}\big)\;\cong\;L^2(f_*\mu).$$

This is the load-bearing factor of the Doob $L^2$ isometry: it transports a function $g$ on the source, measurable for the pulled-back $\sigma$-algebra, to the corresponding function on the image, preserving the $L^2$ norm. The full `doobL2Equiv` is obtained by composing this with Mathlib's $L^2_{\mathrm{meas}}\cong L^2(\mathrm{trim})$ isometry.
